coredata.dc:title: Phase equilibria in the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–X (X: Ti, Zr and Hf) ternary systems
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Miura, Seiji
Ohkubo, Kenji
Terada, Yoshihiro
Kimura, Yoshisato
Mishima, Yoshinao
Yamabe-Mitarai, Yoko
Harada, Hiroshi
Mohri, Tetsuo
coredata.dc:description: Isotherms of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–X (X: Ti, Zr, Hf) systems are presented. In all of the isotherms three-phase equilibrium is found and no ternary compound is observed. Solid solubility of each phase is confirmed by chemical analysis by wavelength dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (WDS). The direction of the solubility lobe of L12–Ir3X with Al addition is explained in terms of the nearest neighbor interactions. Liquidus surfaces are also established by taking into account the microstructure of as-cast alloys, differential thermal analysis (DTA) data, Ir–X binary phase diagrams reported previously and the phase equilibria of the Ir–Al binary system investigated in the present study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intermetallics
Scanning electron microscopy
High temperature alloys

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al binary system
Solid solubility of Al in L12–Ir3X

Present study is partially supported by Grant-in-Aid for Encouragement of Young Scientists from the Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture, Japan, No. 09875163.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ir-rich portion of: (a) Ir–Ti, (b) Ir–Zr and (c) Ir–Hf binary systems redrawn from [28]. L means liquid phase.
Microstructure of as-cast specimens of: (a) Ir–25Al and (b) Ir–35Al. (c) Microstructure of Ir–35Al heat-treated at 1923K for 24h. (d) A partially revised binary Ir–Al phase diagram based on [28].

Microstructure of as-cast specimens of: (a) Ir–24Al–6Ti, (b) Ir–12Al–12Ti and (c) Ir–20Al–10Ti. (d) Liquidus projection of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–Ti ternary system. Here and in the following figures, the symbols, open squares, open circles and open triangles, have the same meanings as in Fig. 2d.
Microstructure of alloys heat-treated at 1923K for 24h: (a) Ir–24Al–6Ti and (b) Ir–12Al–12Ti. (c) Isothermal section of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–Ti ternary system at 1923K.
Microstructure of as-cast specimens of: (a) Ir–20Al–10Zr and (b) Ir–24Al–6Zr. (c) Liquidus projection of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–Zr ternary system.
(a) Microstructure of Ir–24Al–6Zr heat-treated at 1923K for 24h and (b) an isothermal section of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–Zr ternary system at 1923K.
SEM image (a) and area analysis (b) of as-cast Ir–20Al–10Hf specimen. (c) Liquidus projection of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–Hf ternary system.
SEM image (a) and area analysis (b) of Ir–20Al–10Hf heat-treated at 1923K for 24h. (c) Isothermal section of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–Hf ternary system at 1923K.
Comparison between V IrAl/V IrX and V AlX/V IrX of Ir3Ti, Ir3Zr and Ir3Hf with Al addition.
Nominal composition (at.%) and the result of DTA for the binary and ternary alloys investigated

article.ce:title: Phase equilibria in the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–X (X: Ti, Zr and Hf) ternary systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Materials Science and Engineering, Graduate School of Engineering, Hokkaido University, Kita-13, Nishi-8, Kita-ku, Sapporo 060-8628, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgy and Ceramics Science, Tokyo Institute of Technology, Meguro-ku, Tokyo 152-8552, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Tokyo Institute of Technology, 4259 Nagatsuta, Midori-ku, Yokohama 226-8502, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: High Temperature Materials Group, National Institute for Materials Science, Sengen 1-2-1, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-0047, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Isotherms of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–X (X: Ti, Zr, Hf) systems are presented. In all of the isotherms three-phase equilibrium is found and no ternary compound is observed. Solid solubility of each phase is confirmed by chemical analysis by wavelength dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (WDS). The direction of the solubility lobe of L12–Ir3X with Al addition is explained in terms of the nearest neighbor interactions. Liquidus surfaces are also established by taking into account the microstructure of as-cast alloys, differential thermal analysis (DTA) data, Ir–X binary phase diagrams reported previously and the phase equilibria of the Ir–Al binary system investigated in the present study.

ce:section.ce:para: Ir-based alloys have been studied and developed as high temperature materials for special uses such as a container for a radioactive fuel in radioisotope thermoelectric generator heat sources [1,2]. Recently, as a candidate for new heat resisting materials, Yamabe-Mitarai et al. [3–6] have proposed alloys based on high melting-point metals with a face centered cubic (f.c.c.) structure, such as Ir and Rh, with an L12 intermetallic compound as dispersoids, having a similar microstructure as commercial Ni-based superalloys. Ir- and Rh-based L12 compounds also show an excellent high temperature strength [7,8]. Therefore, these alloys are expected for much higher temperature application than Ni-based alloys. In order to obtain a prospect of developing materials, however, not only mechanical and physical properties but also related behavior such as microstructure evolution and oxidation at high temperatures should be understood. Present authors have carried out extensive studies on the physical properties of L12–Rh3X and Ir3X, and B2–IrAl compounds [9–12] and on the mechanical properties of L12–Rh3X compounds [8]. These alloys have a common problem: the quite low sublimation points of Ir and Rh oxides. However, it was reported that both Ir and Rh show a lower penetration rate of oxygen than Al2O3 and SiO2 [13–16]. So far Hosoda et al. demonstrated quite a unique result that the B2 intermetallic compound IrAl and its alloys show good oxidation resistance, originating from the self-organization of both Al2O3 outer-layer and the Ir inner-layer at the alloy surface during oxidation [15–22]. This oxidation-resistant structure repairs by itself through the consumption of IrAl compound, and IrAl coating is expected to be applicable as an oxidation-resistant “smart” coating. A similar finding was also reported on B2–Ir x Ru1−x Al [23]. Besides, B2–IrAl was also reported to exhibit an excellent high temperature strength [24,25].

For the development of these B2–IrAl- or RhAl-based coatings on the proposed Ir- or Rh-based materials, information of the phase equilibria is crucial to avoid the brittle intermediate phases such as the sigma phase in the composition range of concern. Also required is the information on the occurrence of the evolution of microstructures composed of f.c.c. and L12 phases in Ir- and Rh-based alloys caused by Al migration from the coating by diffusion processes. However, only a very limited information on the phase diagram and microstructure evolution is available for the Ir-rich portion of Ir–Al–X and the Rh-rich portion of Rh–Al–X ternary systems with various elements X which form L12 intermetallic compounds with Ir or Rh [26,27]. In order to correlate the phase diagrams with mechanical and physical properties of Ir- and Rh-based alloys, the isothermal sections and liquidus surfaces of the Ir-rich portion of Ir–Al–X (X: Ti, Zr, Hf) ternary systems were investigated in the present study. The direction of the solubility lobe of L12–Ir3X with Al addition was also determined experimentally and explained in terms of the nearest neighbor interactions.

In the present study, all the compositions of alloys will be presented in atomic percent. Fig. 1 shows the Ir-rich portion of the (a) Ir–Ti, (b) Ir–Zr and (c) Ir–Hf binary systems [28]. In the Ir–Ti binary system, a peritectic reaction Liquid (L)+(Ir)ss →Ir3Ti is found at 2388K. The single-phase region of the L12–Ir3Ti phase ranges from 23 to 27% Ti and the solid solubility limit of Ti in (Ir)ss is 10% at 1923K. In the Ir–Zr binary system, there is a eutectic reaction L→(Ir)ss +Ir3Zr at 2393K. The single-phase region of the L12–Ir3Zr phase ranges from 19 to 30% Zr, and the solid solubility limit of Zr in (Ir)ss is 2% at 1923K. In the Ir–Hf binary system, there is also a eutectic reaction L→(Ir)ss +Ir3Hf at 2523K. The single-phase region of the L12–Ir3Hf phase ranges from 22 to 27% Hf, and the solid solubility limit of Hf in (Ir)ss is 5% at 1923K.

The nominal composition for each ternary alloy was chosen by taking the phase relationship in the binary phase diagrams into account, and is listed in Table 1 with the compositions of the binary alloys. Ir–25Al and Ir–35Al alloys were also prepared to examine the Ir-rich side of the Ir–Al binary phase diagram. All specimens with about 10g were prepared from 99.9% Ir, 99.5% Ti, 99.6% Zr, 95% Hf and 99.99% Al by arc-melting several times in an Ar atmosphere on a water-cooled copper hearth. The samples were cut by a wheel cutter and prepared for the observation of the as-cast microstructures. Remaining portions were wrapped in tantalum foil and subjected to a heat treatment for homogenization at 1923K for 24h in an Ar-flow atmosphere. Microstructural observation by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and chemical analysis by wavelength dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (WDS) with pure Ir, Ti, Zr, Hf and Al as references were carried out on mounted and polished specimens. As the scatter of the composition in each phase measured by SEM–WDS was small (<0.1at.%), it is concluded that the heat treatment condition is enough to attain equilibrium. (Ir)ss and Ir3Hf are hard to distinguish by WDS because the atomic number of Hf is close to that of Ir. Therefore, area analysis was conducted to investigate the microstructure in Ir–Al–Hf alloys precisely.

Differential thermal analysis (DTA) specimens with 3mm×3mm×3mm in dimension were also prepared from as-cast specimens, and a Setarum DTA 92 calorimeter was used for the analysis up to 1943K, 20K higher than the homogenization temperature. The heating and cooling rates were 10K/min in an Ar-flow atmosphere. The melting point of pure Ni was measured several times to calibrate the measured temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al binary system
ce:section.ce:para: The eutectic temperature for the Ir–IrAl phase equilibrium is reported to be about 2300K [28,29]. No other reactions were detected by thermal analysis of the binary Ir–35Al specimen up to 1943K in the present study.

Fig. 2 a–c shows SEM images of the as-cast Ir–25Al and Ir–35Al specimens and heat-treated specimen of the binary Ir–35Al alloy, respectively. From the results by WDS for the heat-treated Ir–35Al specimen, the dark area is a B2–IrAl phase with the composition of Ir–51.0Al and the bright phase is a (Ir)ss with the composition of Ir–13.6Al. The dendritic primary phase from the melt is (Ir)ss in the Ir–25Al specimen and the B2–IrAl phase in the Ir–35Al specimen, respectively. Therefore, the Al concentration for the eutectic composition is located between the nominal compositions of both the specimens. The measurement of average composition in the area of lamellar structure was attempted by WDS, and it was determined to be 69.5Ir–30.5Al, presumably corresponding to the composition of the eutectic reaction L→(Ir)ss +IrAl. This is very close to the one reported by the previous works [28,29]. The reported solid solubility of Al in (Ir)ss is higher than that determined by the present study, while the Al concentration of the B2–IrAl phase which is in equilibrium with (Ir)ss is higher than the reported value [30,31]. With these experimental results, the Ir-rich side of the Ir–Al binary phase diagram was slightly revised as shown in Fig. 2d by bold broken lines. The results of composition analysis are also indicated by open circles. This diagram is the basis for the following discussions.

Neither the liquidus projection nor the isothermal section of the Ir–Al–Ti ternary system was reported, and only a ternary compound Al16Ir8Ti6 with a cF116–Mn23Th6-type crystal structure was found previously [26,27]. Fig. 3 a–c shows the microstructure of as-cast specimens containing various amounts of Ti. In Fig. 3a, the dendritic (Ir)ss is surrounded by a lamellar structure composed of (Ir)ss and IrAl. In Fig. 3b, the dendritic primary phase is Ir3Ti which is embraced by a thin bright layer of (Ir)ss and the interdendritic lamellar structure includes B2–IrAl. A similar microstructure is also observed in Fig. 3c. The experimental results are summarized in Fig. 3d. As was shown in Fig. 1a, the Ir–Ti binary system has a peritectic reaction L+(Ir)ss →Ir3Ti. However, the L12–Ir3Ti primary region extends to Ir-rich area as is indicated in Fig. 3b and c. As the lamellar structures contain the IrAl phase in both the alloys, the ternary invariant reaction must be located in the vicinity of these alloy compositions. The Ir–24Al–6Ti alloy shown in Fig. 3a has (Ir)ss dendrites, which suggests (Ir)ss is the primary phase. It is noteworthy that the average composition of the lamellar structure shown in Fig. 3a is found to be located in the Ti-poor side of the line connecting (Ir)ss and IrAl phases as shown in Fig. 3d. This may suggest a ternary peritectic reaction expressed as follows:
(1) L+Ir3Ti→(Ir)ss +IrAl
There was no reaction detected for the Ir–24Al–6Ti alloy by DTA of which composition is close to the ternary peritectic point. This leads to the conclusion that the invariant temperature is higher than 1943K. By taking the binary Ir–Ti and Ir–Al systems into account, the liquidus projection of this ternary system is evaluated as shown in Fig. 3d.

Fig. 4 a and b shows the microstructure of alloys heat-treated at 1923K for 24h. In Fig. 4a, dark and bright regions were determined to be B2–IrAl and (Ir)ss, respectively. A small amount of acicular B2–IrAl precipitates appear in the primary (Ir)ss. In Fig. 4b, similarly with the as-cast alloy shown in Fig. 3b, one sees that dendritic L12–Ir3Ti is covered by a thin layer of (Ir)ss. The dark region is also B2–IrAl and it is observed IrAl also precipitates from the thin (Ir)ss. All the lamellae formed during the solidification were spheroidized by the heat treatment.

From these experimental results and the binary Ir–Ti and Ir–Al systems, an isothermal section at 1923K is evaluated as shown in Fig. 4c. Among all the experimental results, there is no indication of solid phases other than (Ir)ss, Ir3Ti and IrAl phases in the composition range investigated. The composition of Ir3Ti phase in the (Ir)ss–Ir3Ti–IrAl three-phase field strongly suggests that the solubility lobe of L12–Ir3Ti tends to extend along Ir-constant lines. By both experimental and thermodynamic studies, Ochiai et al. [30] showed that the site preference of various third elements X in the unit cell of L12 host compound can be determined from the direction of solubility lobe in the ternary phase diagram. Therefore, it can be concluded that Al substitutes for Ti in the L12–Ir3Ti compound by 6.0%. On the contrary, the solubility of Ti in B2–IrAl is almost zero. The (Ir)ss–Ir3Ti–IrAl three-phase field in Fig. 4c implies that the fraction of L12–Ir3Ti phase in (Ir)ss–Ir3Ti binary two-phase alloys increases with increasing Al concentration through the Al diffusion from the IrAl coating.

Neither the liquidus projection nor the isothermal section of the Ir–Al–Zr ternary system was reported, and only found previously were ternary compounds Al16Ir7Zr6 or Al16Ir8Zr6 with a cF116–Mn23Th6-type crystal structure and AlIrZr with an hP12–MgZn2-type crystal structure [26,27]. Fig. 5 a and b shows the microstructure of as-cast alloys containing different amounts of Zr, and these results are summarized in Fig. 5c. In Fig. 5a, the dark region was determined to be B2–IrAl by WDS analysis, and Ir3Zr has a dendritic morphology and is the primary phase from the melt. The rod-type lamellar structure has a less Zr composition than the line connecting Ir3Zr and IrAl as shown in Fig. 5c. In Fig. 5b, on the other hand, the primary phase is IrAl with a small fraction of (Ir)ss. The fine lamellar structure is composed of (Ir)ss and IrAl. Therefore, the ternary invariant reaction (presumably eutectic) involving the liquid, IrAl, (Ir)ss and Ir3Zr phases is expected to be located in the vicinity of the compositions of each alloys. From these experimental results and the binary Ir–Zr and Ir–Al systems, the liquidus projection is evaluated and shown semi-schematically in Fig. 5c.

Fig. 6 a shows the microstructure of Ir–24Al–6Zr alloy heat-treated at 1923K for 24h. The dark regions are determined to be B2–IrAl. The gray and bright regions are Ir3Zr and (Ir)ss, respectively. All the lamellae formed during the solidification were spheroidized by the heat treatment as was seen in the Ir–Al–Ti alloys. From these experimental results and the binary Ir–Zr and Ir–Al systems, an isothermal section at 1923K is evaluated as shown in Fig. 6b. Among all the experimental results, there is no indication of solid phases other than (Ir)ss, Ir3Zr and IrAl in the composition range investigated. A ternary compound AlIrZr with an hP12–MgZn2-type crystal structure is not shown while it appears at 773K [26]. The composition of the Ir3Zr phase in the (Ir)ss–Ir3Zr–IrAl three-phase field strongly suggests that the solubility lobe of L12–Ir3Zr have a tendency to extend along Ir-constant lines. Therefore, it can be concluded that Al substitutes for Zr in Ir3Zr by 7.4%. On the other hand, the solubility of Zr in B2–IrAl is also very small as was seen in the Ir–Al–Ti system. With the IrAl coating, the fraction of L12–Ir3Zr phase in (Ir)ss–Ir3Zr binary two-phase alloys should increase through the Al diffusion.

Neither the liquidus projection nor the isothermal section of the Ir–Al–Hf ternary system was reported, and only found previously were ternary compounds Al16Hf6Ir7 or Al16Hf6Ir8 with a cF116–Mn23Th6-type crystal structure and AlHfIr with an hP12–MgZn2-type crystal structure [26,27]. Fig. 7 a shows the microstructure of as-cast Ir–20Al–10Hf alloy. The dark region was identified as B2–IrAl by WDS analysis. However, it is hard to distinguish (Ir)ss and Ir3Hf from SEM images because the atomic number of Hf is close to that of Ir. Area analysis for the elements was performed for the determination of phases and the results are shown in Fig. 7b. There are blocky Ir3Hf and (Ir)ss phases, and both (Ir)ss–IrAl and Ir3Hf–IrAl lamellar structures also appear. Ir3Hf seems to be a primary phase from the melt. The lamellar structure composed of Ir3Hf and IrAl phases suggests that a solidification sequence in this alloy is L→L+Ir3Hf→L+Ir3Hf+IrAl. Then, the existence of (Ir)ss–IrAl lamellar structure implies there is a ternary peritectic invariant reaction:
(2) L+Ir3Hf→(Ir)ss +IrAl
followed by a eutectic monovariant line L→(Ir)ss +IrAl. Therefore, the extended line from Ir3Hf to the alloy composition (Ir–20Al–10Hf) must intersect with L+Ir3Hf+IrAl monovariant line and the peritectic point should be located in the Ir-rich side of the extended line.

From these experimental results and the phase equilibria of binary Ir–Hf and Ir–Al systems, the liquidus projection is evaluated as shown in Fig. 7c. The ternary peritectic composition should be at the Hf-poor side of the line connecting (Ir)ss and IrAl, and the invariant temperature must be higher than that of the invariant reaction L→(Ir)ss +IrAl in the binary Ir–Al system. There was no reaction detected in the Ir–20Al–10Hf alloy by DTA, which is consistent with the DTA result of the binary Ir–Al alloys.

Fig. 8 a shows the microstructure of Ir–20Al–10Hf alloy heat-treated at 1923K for 24h. Area analysis shown in Fig. 8b enables to obtain further insights. All the lamellae formed during the solidification are also spheroidized by the heat treatment. From these experimental results and the binary Ir–Hf and Ir–Al systems, an isothermal section at 1923K is evaluated as shown in Fig. 8c. Among all the experimental results, there is no indication of solid phases other than (Ir)ss, Ir3Hf and IrAl phases in the composition range investigated. A ternary compound AlIrHf with an hP12–MgZn2-type crystal structure is not shown while it appears at 773K [26]. By comparing the composition of Ir3Hf phase in the (Ir)ss–Ir3Hf–IrAl three-phase field with the stoichiometric composition of Ir3Hf binary alloy at which a congruent melting occurs, it can be concluded that Al substitutes for Hf in the Ir3Hf ternary phase at 1923K by 5.1%. The solubility of Hf in B2–IrAl is also very small as were seen in the Ir–Al–Ti and Ir–Al–Zr systems. Contrary to these ternary systems, with the IrAl coating, the fraction of L12–Ir3Hf phase in (Ir)ss–Ir3Hf binary two-phase alloys would be kept almost constant through the Al diffusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid solubility of Al in L12–Ir3X
ce:section.ce:para:
The solubility limits of various elements in L12–Ni3X (X: Al, Si, Ga, Ge) were determined experimentally and the direction of solubility lobe was explained within the nearest neighbor interaction model [30]. Similarly, the substitution behavior of elements in Al3Li with L12 crystal structure and Al3Nb with D022 crystal structure were also predicted by the pseudo-ground state analysis [31,32]. In all ternary systems investigated in the present study, there is a common tendency that a significant amount of Al substitutes for a constituting element X of each L12–Ir3X phases. Ochiai et al. [32] showed that a simple energetics consideration provides an explanation of the substitution behavior in L12–Ni3X (X: Al, Si, Ga, Ge). They found that following expression is fulfilled if an element Y substitutes for an X site
(3)
V NiY/V NiX > V XY/V NiX + P
where V NiX, V NiY and V XY are interaction parameters which can be expressed by V NiX = H NiX −(H NiNi + H XX)/2, etc., and P is a parameter which depends on the pair interaction energies H NiNi, H XX and H NiX. In this study, we apply this evaluation method for understanding the substitution behavior of Al in Ir3X (X: Ti, Zr, Hf) with parameters estimated from Miedema's semi-empirical formula [33]. Fig. 9 indicates the correlation between V IrAl/V IrX and V AlX/V IrX. All the plots are located on the straight line with a slope of unity. Both the values for V IrAl/V IrX and V AlX/V IrX are smaller than unity, which means the effective pair interaction between Ir and X is stronger than those between Al and Ir and between Al and X. According to the evaluation of solubility of elements in Ni-based L12 compounds by Ochiai et al. [30], the values for V IrAl/V IrX of Ir3X in which Al substitutes for Ir should be almost zero, whereas V IrAl/V IrX and V AlX/V IrX are almost the same when Al substitutes for X. Although the P value must be different in each Ir3X compounds, the values of V IrAl/V IrX and V AlX/V IrX are almost the same for each compound as shown in Fig. 9. From this point of view, it also can be rationalized that Al substitutes for Ti, Zr and Hf in Ir3X (X: Ti, Zr, Hf) phase.

The liquidus projections and isotherms of the Ir-rich portion of the Ir–Al–X (X: Ti, Zr, Hf) containing f.c.c.-Ir solid solution (Ir)ss, Ir-based L12 intermetallic compounds Ir3X and B2–IrAl are proposed. In all of the isotherms f.c.c./L12/B2 three-phase equilibrium is found and no ternary compound is observed. The solid solubility of each phase is confirmed by WDS analysis, and it was shown that the direction of the solubility lobe of L12–Ir3X with Al addition can be interpreted within the nearest neighbor interaction model.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the technical assistance of Mr. N. Miyazaki of Hokkaido University. Present study is partially supported by Grant-in-Aid for Encouragement of Young Scientists from the Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture, Japan, No. 09875163.

E.L. Courtright, Ultra high temperature carbon/carbon composite assessment study, WRDC, US Air Force, Report WL-TR-91-4061, September 1992.
G.R. St. Pierre, in: R. Tanaka, et al. (Eds.), Proceedings of the Third International Symposium on Ultra-high Temperature Materials’93, JUTEMI, Tajimi, 1993, p. 22.
